text:
Centre for Epilepsy

Neurosciences Division

Clinic Date: 12 September 2025

Dr Sarah Nguyen
Consultant Neurologist
Centre for Epilepsy
St Thomas’ Hospital, London

Dear Dr Sarah Nguyen,

Mr Alex Turner, DOB: 22-03-1991, Hospital No: A784210 NHS No. 9083774219
Flat 12, 45 Riverside Walk, London, SE1 7DG

Diagnosis:

Generalised epilepsy with a partial response to Valproate; Topiramate discontinued due to cognitive side effects; currently on Valproate with adjunctive Clobazam as needed.

Aetiology:

Idiopathic generalised epilepsy phenotype; no structural lesion identified on MRI; normal metabolic screen; family history negative for epilepsy.

Other Medical Problems:

Mild asthma, well controlled with infrequent salbutamol use. Vitamin D insufficiency corrected; latest level within range.

Management Plan:

Continue Sodium Valproate 500 mg mane and 1000 mg nocte. Maintain single-brand supply. Clobazam 10 mg as rescue per existing plan. I will arrange serum valproate and LFTs prior to next review. Follow-up in six months, sooner if clinically required.

Clinical Summary:

Mr Alex Turner reports that seizures are occurring with a clustering pattern, most often every 2 - 3 days, with occasional short periods of increased frequency. Rescue medication has been needed on 3 occasions this year. No clear progression compared to previous follow-up. He describes typical events as brief generalised myoclonic jerks in the mornings and occasional absence episodes later in the day; there have been no generalised tonic–clonic seizures for over 18 months. Sleep regularity and hydration appear to influence symptom fluctuation. He commutes mainly by bicycle and notes that clusters tend to occur on weeks with heavier training rides, although this is patient-reported and not yet systematically tracked. No recent hospital admissions.

Investigations:

EEG (May 2025) demonstrated generalised 3–4 Hz spike-and-wave discharges consistent with generalised epilepsy. MRI brain (2024) unremarkable. Routine blood tests in June 2025 normal including LFTs, FBC, and U&E.

Discussion:

We discussed ongoing generalised epilepsy management. Given the stable pattern without clear progression and acceptable tolerability of Valproate, I have not altered baseline therapy. He will continue to use Clobazam as rescue under the established plan. I encouraged continued seizure diary recording, particularly around cycling intensity, sleep, and caffeine intake, to help inform future optimisation.

Yours sincerely,

entities.text: seizures are occurring with a clustering pattern, most often every 2 - 3 days

Seizures are occurring with a clustering pattern, most often every 2 - 3 days, with occasional short periods of increased frequency. Rescue medication has been needed on 3 occasions this year. No clear progression compared to previous follow-up

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: Identify explicit seizure frequency statements. The Clinical Summary states: 'seizures are occurring with a clustering pattern, most often every 2 - 3 days, with occasional short periods of increased frequency.' This provides a clear numeric range for occurrence frequency. No other higher-frequency figure is provided. There are no generalised tonic–clonic seizures for over 18 months, but myoclonic jerks and absence episodes are ongoing, so not seizure-free. Apply the rule to select the highest frequency; the explicit range 'every 2 - 3 days' maps to '1 per 2 to 3 day'. No need to convert to cluster format as per allowed forms unless cluster size is specified, which it isn't.
seizure_frequency_number: seizures are occurring with a clustering pattern, most often every 2 - 3 days
reference: Seizures are occurring with a clustering pattern, most often every 2 - 3 days, with occasional short periods of increased frequency. Rescue medication has been needed on 3 occasions this year. No clear progression compared to previous follow-up
